title: Dead Guy In Room 4

jokes.text: John enthusiastically declares 'tidal waves of prosperity are crashing down, all around us' while clearly standing in a modest, struggling small-town motel
jokes.setup: John optimistically announces 'looks like another full house tonight' with genuine excitement
jokes.punchline: Moira responds with elaborate, theatrical sarcasm — 'tidal waves of prosperity are crashing down, all around us. Soon enough. You just wait.' — dripping with contempt for their reduced circumstances
jokes.explanation: A solid opener that immediately establishes both the show's central tension and Moira's character voice. The elaborate vocabulary ('tidal waves of prosperity') applied to their modest circumstances is perfectly Moira. Strong character integration — only she would respond this way.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We are on the cusp of something extraordinary. Tidal waves of prosperity are about to crash down upon this establishment.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Johnny, it's a motel in a small town.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Precisely. Tidal waves.

jokes.text: Stevie flatly reports 'There's a dead guy in Room 4' to John's cheerful greeting of 'Hey partner, what's the good word?'
jokes.setup: John opens with a breezy, upbeat 'Hey partner, what's the good word?' after his optimistic morning mood
jokes.punchline: Stevie's completely flat, matter-of-fact 'There's a dead guy in Room 4' — no alarm, no emotion, just information delivery
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes and also the cold open punchline for the entire episode. Structurally perfect: the cheerful setup ('what's the good word?') is completely demolished by the maximum-stakes flat delivery. Economy is nearly flawless — six words that are also the episode title. Stevie's deadpan is completely character-specific. This earns its 8+ score.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, hello! You look like you have some good news for me.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I don't. The motel is being audited by the IRS, and we're also being sued by that guest who slipped in the parking lot.

jokes.text: Moira: 'I have endured a cornucopia of trauma the last few years, I draw the line at living in a crime scene.'
jokes.setup: Family is reacting to news of a dead body; John clarifies no one has been murdered
jokes.punchline: Moira dramatically escalates to declaring she 'draws the line' at living in a crime scene — as if she's been nobly enduring everything else
jokes.explanation: 'Cornucopia of trauma' is peak Moira vocabulary — Latinate, theatrical, completely overwrought for the situation. The joke is in the word choice ('cornucopia') and the absurd specificity of where she draws her line. Very Moira-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A cornucopia of trauma.

jokes.text: Stevie describes the dead man as 'like, asleep in his bed. But like, forever asleep. Like, checked out without paying, asleep.'
jokes.setup: John demands to know what she means by 'dead guy'
jokes.punchline: Three-stage deadpan escalation that lands on a motel-specific pun — 'checked out without paying' — as a euphemism for dying
jokes.explanation: The three-part escalating euphemism structure is exceptionally well-crafted. Each iteration builds: 'asleep' → 'forever asleep' → 'checked out without paying, asleep.' The motel-specific wordplay in the final beat is genuinely clever and specific to Stevie's character and setting. This earns its high score — the callback to motel language is witty, not cheap.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: He checked out.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Permanently.

jokes.text: John: 'I think it's a little early for humor, Stevie.' Stevie: 'Oh, I'm sorry, did you just see a dead body?'
jokes.setup: John chides Stevie for making death puns when they've just discovered a dead guest
jokes.punchline: Stevie's retort implying SHE was the one who found the body and therefore has more right to cope through dark humor
jokes.explanation: Sharp, economical retort. Stevie dismantles John's moral authority in eight words by pointing out the asymmetry of their situations. The dark logic is airtight and the delivery would be bone-dry. Very good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I don't think that's appropriate humor for a comedy show.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's a dead guy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Exactly. Have a little respect.

jokes.text: Moira: 'What did this gentleman look like?' Stevie: 'I don't know, like old.' Moira: 'John old? Or old, old?'
jokes.setup: Moira asks about the deceased's appearance, ostensibly to identify him
jokes.punchline: Moira's distinction between 'John old' and 'old, old' reveals she's using the dead man's age as a vanity reference point for her husband
jokes.explanation: Brilliant economy. The joke reveals Moira's narcissism (she's worried about John's age in the context of a dead man) in just four words: 'John old? Or old, old?' The character integration is near-perfect — this specific vanity concern is entirely Moira. It's economical, layered, and quotable. Earns the 8+.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You're making jokes? This is serious.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, I'm the one who found the body, so I think I've earned the right to cope however I want.

jokes.text: John: 'I don't quite see the relevance in that question, Moira, the man is dead.'
jokes.setup: Moira asks whether the dead man was 'John old or old, old'
jokes.punchline: John's weary, measured correction — pointing out that the man's comparative age is irrelevant given he is deceased
jokes.explanation: A solid straight-man beat. John's weary rationality is a good foil to Moira's absurdity. Functional but not exceptional — the joke has already landed with Moira's line, this is just cleanup.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: How old was he?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Because if he was younger than Johnny, I'm going to be very upset.

jokes.text: David overhears 'there's a dead body in one of the rooms' and immediately declares 'I always knew there'd be a murder here eventually. I'm gonna go pack up my things, I assume we're moving?'
jokes.setup: The family is managing the crisis of a guest dying of natural causes
jokes.punchline: David immediately catastrophizes to 'murder' and assumes the entire family is relocating — two logical leaps that reveal his dramatic character
jokes.explanation: Good character work for David. 'I always knew there'd be a murder here' suggests he's been living in low-grade dread about the motel, and the immediate 'I assume we're moving?' skips all intermediate steps. Solid character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, a man is dead. Can we please focus on that instead of my age?

jokes.text: Moira: 'Why do I find that scenario even more bone-chilling than murder?'
jokes.setup: John clarifies the old man died peacefully alone in his sleep — not murdered
jokes.punchline: Moira finds peaceful, solitary death MORE disturbing than murder — a revealing admission about her existential fears
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny and character-revealing. Moira's terror of dying alone (and perhaps obscurely) is perfectly consistent with her theatrical personality. The word 'bone-chilling' applied to peaceful death is funny and a little poignant. This line has surprising depth for a quick one-liner.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, at a motel? I'm surprised there's only one body.

jokes.text: John asks 'Stevie, do we know how this man expired?' Stevie says 'Do I look like a coroner?' John replies 'I don't think you want people answering that question.'
jokes.setup: Stevie deflects with 'Do I look like a coroner?'
jokes.punchline: John's gentle implication that Stevie's appearance might actually invite the comparison — a rare moment of John getting the better of Stevie
jokes.explanation: A clean two-beat joke. Stevie walks into it with her rhetorical question, and John delivers a mild but well-timed burn. The surprise of John being the one to land a comedic hit adds a layer. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A natural death? How pedestrian. I find it far more disturbing than murder, actually. At least with murder there's passion, drama, a narrative arc. But to simply... expire in one's sleep? It's so undignified. So... final.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'It feels like every time the motel sells out, someone dies.' John: 'Uh, we've only sold out one night.' Stevie: 'Exactly.'
jokes.setup: Stevie observes a pattern between full motel bookings and death
jokes.punchline: John's correction that they've only sold out once — which Stevie weaponizes to confirm the pattern is 100% consistent (1 for 1)
jokes.explanation: The best joke in the episode. The structure is mathematically perfect: Stevie sets up what sounds like an exaggeration, John tries to correct it, and his correction CONFIRMS the pattern with 100% statistical accuracy. 'Exactly.' is the single-word kill shot. Perfect economy — the joke requires nothing more. The logic is airtight and the comedic payoff is explosive. This is genuinely exceptional joke construction.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Well, he died. I assume the usual way—his heart stopped beating, his brain stopped functioning, and he became very, very still.

jokes.text: David: 'Fall off a bridge, please.' (response to Alexis pressing him about the kiss with Patrick)
jokes.setup: Alexis is interrogating David about whether Patrick explicitly told him he wanted the kiss
jokes.punchline: David's theatrical, over-the-top-but-casual dismissal: 'Fall off a bridge, please.'
jokes.explanation: Deeply David. The politeness of 'please' attached to a violent request is his comedic signature — extreme sentiment delivered with mannered precision. The joke is in the juxtaposition. Highly quotable and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You know what's weird? Every time a band sells out, someone dies.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's... not a pattern, Stevie.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Isn't it though? Think about it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm not going to think about it, because it's insane.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: We sold out once.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: The Crows have sold out exactly one time. And someone died.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Stevie, that's not—

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Your mother almost died that time.

jokes.text: Alexis accuses David of 'French-ing your business partner literally the second he peeks his head out of the closet'
jokes.setup: Alexis chides David for getting involved with Patrick after criticizing her relationship with Ted
jokes.punchline: 'peeks his head out of the closet' — literal (Patrick coming out as gay) and figurative (coming out of the store closet/room) double meaning, delivered with Alexis's breezy obliviousness
jokes.explanation: The phrasing works on multiple levels — Patrick's coming out as gay and the literal sense of emerging from somewhere. The word 'French-ing' is also a funny choice, more juvenile than Alexis probably intended. The line has charm and layers.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I didn't kiss him. He kissed me. There's a very big difference, and frankly, I'm insulted that you would even suggest otherwise.

jokes.text: David: 'I'll have you know this is the healthiest first day of a relationship I've ever had.'
jokes.setup: Alexis calls out David for kissing his business partner
jokes.punchline: David's sincere declaration that this chaotic situation represents his personal best for relationship health — a devastating self-indictment delivered with pride
jokes.explanation: This joke works beautifully because it requires knowledge of David's chaotic romantic history while also being funny on its face. The earnestness with which he delivers it is the comedic payload — he's genuinely proud of something that still sounds objectively messy. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, David kissed Patrick? Like, he full-on French kissed him?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, Alexis, I French kissed him. That's what people do when they're attracted to someone.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know what French kissing is, David. I'm just... so you're really doing this? You're coming out?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, I'm coming out. And yes, there will be French involved.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Patrick is a sweet little button face, David, so don't mess this up.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is weighing in on David's new relationship with Patrick
jokes.punchline: 'Sweet little button face' — an absurdly specific, slightly infantilizing physical description that Alexis deploys with complete sincerity as a compliment
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'sweet little button face' is a uniquely Alexis construction — affectionate but slightly condescending, visually specific, and totally earnest. The callback when Moira says it later elevates it further.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, he's such a sweet little button face.

jokes.text: John's flat delivery of: 'It'll take a day just to re-box your mother's wigs.'
jokes.setup: David suggests the new guests could stay in John and Moira's room as a solution
jokes.punchline: John's practical objection reveals the absurdity of Moira's wig collection being so extensive it would take a full day to deal with
jokes.explanation: A well-observed character detail deployed efficiently. The wig collection is an established Moira trait and 're-boxing' implies it's a significant logistical undertaking. Good economy, strong character specificity.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Oh my God, can you imagine? Not one of you is trained.'
jokes.setup: John mentions it would take a day to re-box Moira's wigs if they had to vacate their room
jokes.punchline: Moira is horrified at the prospect of untrained family members handling her wigs — a completely delusional level of specialization implied
jokes.explanation: The idea that handling Moira's wigs requires specialized training is peak absurdist character comedy. 'Not one of you is trained' implies a professional certification exists for this. Excellent character integration — only Moira would say this and mean it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: They can stay in your room.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Our room? Where are we supposed to put Moira's wigs?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We'll make it work.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Make it work? Moira, they're going to be sitting on the wigs.

jokes.text: David's response to being told to pack up and leave his room: 'Okay, every inch of that sentence made me sick.'
jokes.setup: John tells the family they need to vacate their rooms for motel guests, explaining that a dead body in Room 4 means they need David and Alexis's rooms.
jokes.punchline: David's total-body revulsion expressed as a measured critical review of the sentence — 'every inch of that sentence made me sick' — is both a visceral reaction and a weirdly formal critique.
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'every inch of that sentence' is a standout David-ism. The physical metaphor applied to language ('inch') is unexpected and precise. It's quotable and entirely his.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Re-boxing? Do you have any idea what's involved in the storage and handling of a collection of this magnitude? These aren't just wigs, Johnny. They're architectural feats. They require climate control, acid-free tissue paper, and hands that have been professionally trained in delicate fabric manipulation.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I have spent years cultivating relationships with my wig technician. She knows their individual needs, their personalities, their histories. You can't just hand them over to some... some untrained person and expect them to emerge unscathed.

jokes.text: David: 'I've never just thrown some things in a bag before, so you might have to give me a minute.'
jokes.setup: John tells the family to 'throw some things in a bag' and move rooms
jokes.punchline: David treats the casual instruction as if it's a complex, unfamiliar skill he'll need time to learn
jokes.explanation: Efficient delivery of David's former wealth and current incompetence at basic tasks. The 'you might have to give me a minute' makes it funnier — he's not refusing, just genuinely requesting patience for an unfamiliar procedure.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't know how to pack casually. I have a very specific packing system.

jokes.text: Alexis, David, and then Alexis again quickly establish they'd rather sleep 'somewhere else,' 'literally anywhere else,' and 'like an old tent by the side of the highway' than in their parents' room
jokes.setup: John announces the kids will move into his and Moira's room
jokes.punchline: Three-beat escalation of rejection culminating in 'old tent by the side of the highway' — increasingly drastic alternatives that reveal how much they dread proximity to their parents
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed escalating bit. The specificity of 'old tent by the side of the highway' — not just a tent, an OLD tent, on a HIGHWAY — is the cherry on top. Good rhythm from the siblings trading lines.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I guess I'll sleep in the car.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'll sleep in the office.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I suppose I could manage the living room.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh, well in that case, I'll sleep in a roadside tent. Alone. In the dark. Where anything could happen.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Unfortunately my previous engagements preclude me from offering my beneficence around the motel today.' John, flatly: 'Yeah, I know, Moira, you know, busy busy.'
jokes.setup: The family is in crisis mode; Moira is clearly going to dodge the work
jokes.punchline: Moira's outrageously Latinate excuse ('beneficence,' 'preclude,' 'engagements') is deflated by John's resigned, exhausted 'busy busy' — two words that say everything about their marriage
jokes.explanation: The contrast between Moira's elaborate vocabulary and John's deflated two-word response is a comedic rhythm this show does brilliantly. 'Beneficence' is specifically funny because it's the wrong word choice — she means 'help' but uses a word implying charitable generosity. John's 'busy busy' implies long-term acceptance of her avoidance.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I would love to help, but I'm afraid I have a very strict policy about involving myself in any situation that could potentially result in me having to wear comfortable shoes or speak to a police officer without proper lighting.

jokes.text: Stevie asks for 'a roll of dry-cleaning bags and some padlocks' after everyone leaves, clearly preparing for some grim task related to the dead body
jokes.setup: Crisis has been established; everyone is scrambling
jokes.punchline: Stevie's extremely specific, darkly practical supply request — dry-cleaning bags and padlocks — implies a disturbing but unstated plan for managing the situation
jokes.explanation: The joke is entirely in what's NOT said. The combination of dry-cleaning bags (body-sized plastic) and padlocks in the context of a dead guest is darkly hilarious. The specificity makes it funnier — she's clearly already formulated a plan. Economy is excellent — she says two items and the audience fills in the terrifying blanks.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'll help you pack, but I need to be very clear about something. Everything must be folded using the KonMari method. All hangers must face the same direction. Shoes need to be stored in their original boxes, organized by season and then by color gradient. And absolutely no mixing of natural and synthetic fibers in the same suitcase.

jokes.text: John: 'Wait, you told Roland?' Stevie: 'Yes... I don't know, I ran into him, and he asked me how I was.'
jokes.setup: John learns Roland has been told about the dead body
jokes.punchline: Stevie's explanation — she told Roland because he asked 'how she was' — implies she gave Roland a full situation report in response to a casual pleasantry
jokes.explanation: A nice bit of character comedy. The implication that 'how are you?' prompted a complete crisis disclosure is funny and establishes Stevie's tendency to overshare under pressure. The 'I might have panicked' follow-up seals it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Stevie told Roland? About the body? Why would she do that?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I mean, first it was the chopsticks incident, then it was the whole gas leak situation, and now we're just casually discussing corpse disposal with the town real estate agent?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We've gone from 'Did anyone see my good chopsticks?' to 'Has anyone seen my good body disposal expert?' This is what happens when you lower your standards.

jokes.text: John: 'I wouldn't trust Roland with a set of chopsticks, let alone a dead body!'
jokes.setup: Stevie has told Roland about the dead guest; Roland has offered help via a coroner contact
jokes.punchline: John's extreme simile establishing Roland's untrustworthiness — beginning with something harmless (chopsticks) and escalating to the specific high-stakes situation at hand
jokes.explanation: The 'chopsticks' comparison is a good specific choice — mundane but requiring some dexterity — which makes the escalation to 'dead body' funnier. Well-structured comparison joke that works without requiring context.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: We've got a situation. The... the toilet has stopped working, and we need an autopsy to figure out what's wrong with it.

jokes.text: Roland loudly announces to the lobby — including a guest checking out — that 'the toilet that died in Room 4... the plumber will be here in a few hours. He said he'd be here sooner, but he has another autopsy.'
jokes.setup: John and Stevie are desperate to keep the death secret; Roland offers to use his code word in front of a guest
jokes.punchline: Roland's code word is 'toilet that died' — absurdly transparent — AND he breaks cover immediately by mentioning the plumber also has 'another autopsy'
jokes.explanation: This is a masterclass in cringe comedy. Roland's 'code' manages to be (1) completely non-disguising of the situation and (2) self-undermining within the same sentence. The 'he has another autopsy' punchline destroys any pretense while Roland remains oblivious. The structure is perfect — setup of code, immediate demolition of code. Earns the high score.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I don't think they should see it. I mean, I saw my father get taken out of our house when I was nine years old, and I turned out fine.

jokes.text: John's mortified, weak response: 'Toilet on the fritz.'
jokes.setup: Roland has just announced 'autopsy' to the lobby in front of a guest
jokes.punchline: John desperately tries to maintain the cover story with 'toilet on the fritz' — hopelessly late and inadequate
jokes.explanation: John's desperate attempt to salvage the cover after Roland has already blown it. The comedy is in the futility — three words trying to undo a catastrophically revealing announcement. Works as a callback to Roland's terrible code word.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Hmm.

jokes.text: Roland: 'I saw a lot of dead bodies when I was a kid, und I would say that I turned out pretty okay.'
jokes.setup: Roland suggests keeping guests in their rooms won't traumatize them
jokes.punchline: Roland's childhood dead body exposure as evidence of resilience — completely undermined by the audience's awareness that Roland is NOT, in fact, 'pretty okay'
jokes.explanation: The joke operates on dramatic irony: Roland's self-assessment ('turned out pretty okay') is hilariously contradicted by everything the audience knows about him. Also darkly funny that he saw 'a lot' of dead bodies as a child with no elaboration. The 'und' is a nice character touch suggesting some regional dialect/background.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, why does everyone here look so sad? Is it because their pets are dying?

jokes.text: John's single 'Hmm.' in response to Roland claiming he turned out 'pretty okay'
jokes.setup: Roland has just claimed he turned out 'pretty okay' despite a childhood full of dead bodies
jokes.punchline: John's one-syllable, deeply loaded non-response — the most polite possible expression of profound disagreement
jokes.explanation: One word. One syllable. Infinite implication. John's 'Hmm' is loaded with years of Roland endurance and politely suppressed judgment. The economy score is perfect — you cannot do more with less. It's funnier than any actual retort would be.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I didn't quit, I just realized that position wasn't utilizing my full potential. It was actually a really healthy decision for my career growth.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: But yeah, it was super fun. I loved it there.

jokes.text: Alexis enters the vet clinic and surveys the sad applicants: 'Um, was there like a pet massacre, or something? What is with all these sad looking people?'
jokes.setup: Ted is conducting job interviews for Alexis's replacement at the vet clinic
jokes.punchline: Alexis immediately assumes the sad-looking job applicants are grieving pet owners — a complete misread of the situation
jokes.explanation: Alexis's obliviousness to the idea that people looking for work might appear anxious/sad is good character work. The phrase 'pet massacre' is comically excessive. Solid but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I have to be honest with you guys. When I worked at the vet clinic, I had absolutely no interest in animals.

jokes.text: Ted clarifies the applicants are 'perfectly happy-looking people' applying for Alexis's old job
jokes.setup: Alexis just called the applicants 'sad looking'
jokes.punchline: Ted gently reframes them as 'perfectly happy-looking' — the irony being they obviously aren't, and Ted's mild defensiveness implies he feels guilty about replacing Alexis
jokes.explanation: A minor beat. Ted's diplomatic reframing is gentle irony but doesn't land as a strong joke — it's more character texture than comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I... I don't think that's a good idea.

jokes.text: Alexis insists on the word 'quit' having no 'negative connotation' given that they 'had a pretty fun time working together'
jokes.setup: Ted corrects Alexis's use of the word 'quit' by noting the negative connotation
jokes.punchline: Alexis reframes quitting as a positive because their time together was fun — completely missing that quitting is still quitting, and implicitly equating professional departure with the end of their relationship
jokes.explanation: Mild irony in Alexis's logic. The humor is in the gap between her breezy reframing and the reality of the situation. More character texture than an outright joke.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Bzzzzt!

jokes.text: Alexis addresses the waiting job applicants: 'I got a job here, and I have literally zero interest in animals, so you guys are already like, way ahead.'
jokes.setup: Ted has allowed Alexis to sit in on the job interviews
jokes.punchline: Alexis 'encourages' the applicants by revealing her own complete unsuitability for the job she just vacated — inadvertently damning herself while ostensibly complimenting them
jokes.explanation: Alexis's oblivious self-undermining is excellent character comedy. She means this as encouragement but it's a confession that she was spectacularly unqualified. The delivery would be breezy and sincere. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm afraid I'm far too preoccupied with my various endeavors to lend myself to such pedestrian pursuits.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Between my sartorial machinations, my quotidian ablutions, and my nebulous creative consultations, I simply haven't the temporal bandwidth.

jokes.text: Patrick, clearly nervous: 'Could not sleep, been thinking about... stuff, you know, last night.' Long pause. 'Regrets?'
jokes.setup: Patrick admits he's been up since 5am thinking about the previous night's kiss
jokes.punchline: David immediately jumps to 'Regrets?' as if that's the obvious next word — revealing his own insecurity while the question hangs awkwardly
jokes.explanation: The comedic beat is David's almost reflexive self-undermining — jumping to the worst possible interpretation. The word 'Regrets?' as a single-word follow-up to 'stuff' is perfect. Also reads as a character moment revealing David's emotional defenses.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Can I get you some tea?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, that would be lovely. Something herbal, perhaps?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Of course. Chamomile, peppermint, or ginger?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Surprise me. Shock me. Do something unexpected.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I'll just grab you a chamomile.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No, no, no. I said surprise me, Peter.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: It's Patrick.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, I know your name.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'I don't know, I think it's a, just a habit to ask.'
jokes.setup: David is baffled that Patrick would ask if he has regrets
jokes.punchline: Patrick's gentle, slightly amused explanation that asking about regrets is his 'habit' — implying his previous relationships regularly ended with post-mortem regret discussions
jokes.explanation: A quiet but effective joke. The implication that Patrick has been in enough bad relationships to develop a post-kiss 'regrets?' routine is darkly funny and reveals character backstory economically.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I apologize for interrupting, but I think I may have killed someone.

jokes.text: David asks Patrick if he can stay at his place, framing it as 'some things happening at the motel' — Patrick responds with evident panic that he needs 'to take this a lot slower than a sleepover tonight'
jokes.setup: David innocently asks to stay at Patrick's place due to the motel chaos
jokes.punchline: Patrick misreads the request as a sexual advance and panics — the misdirection being that David's request was purely practical
jokes.explanation: Nice misdirection that also does character work for both men — David's obliviousness and Patrick's nerves about his new relationship. The phrase 'a sleepover sleepover' in David's backpedaling is a charming detail. The misunderstanding feels earned rather than contrived.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: When you're in a crisis, you have to treat yourself. It's very important for your mental health.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Even if you've killed someone?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Especially then, David.

jokes.text: Moira sweeps into the store mid-conversation as Patrick is trying to make an exit; Stevie (presumably Patrick) makes an excuse to go 'to the back'
jokes.setup: David and Patrick are in the middle of an awkward, intimate conversation
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'Ding-a-ding' entrance — completely oblivious, immediately dominating the space — while Patrick grabs something and retreats
jokes.explanation: Moira's 'Ding-a-ding' is a perfectly absurd self-announcement. The comic timing of her entrance killing the intimate moment is well-executed. Functional scene transition humor.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I didn't have any pain medication to give him. I only had sample packets from the pharmacy, and I was too embarrassed to admit that.

jokes.text: Moira claims she is 'positively bedeviled with meetings, etc.' — her cover for avoiding motel work
jokes.setup: David notes the store is quiet and wonders why Moira couldn't help their father at the motel
jokes.punchline: Moira's non-specific 'etc.' tacked onto 'positively bedeviled with meetings' — she can't even complete the lie convincingly
jokes.explanation: The 'etc.' is the tell — a person actually bedeviled with meetings could enumerate them. The Latinate 'bedeviled' with the trailing 'etc.' is a perfect Moira construction. Character-specific humor.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm absolutely mortified. I've been taking medication samples from my dermatologist like some sort of... pharmaceutical dumpster diver. And the worst part? I've been telling people they're *boluses*. A bolus! As if I'm some sort of medical professional administering carefully measured doses, when really I'm just... popping pills like they're Tic Tacs at a SoulCycle class.

jokes.text: Moira asks for 'a tea,' David says 'we don't sell tea,' Patrick offers to make a run to the café — Moira: 'No, that's not necessary. How serendipitous. Thank you, Peter.'
jokes.setup: Patrick offers to get Moira a tea from the café despite David's objection
jokes.punchline: Moira thanks Patrick by calling him 'Peter' — immediately getting his name wrong and framing it as a magical coincidence ('how serendipitous')
jokes.explanation: Moira calling Patrick 'Peter' is a running gag setup moment. The 'how serendipitous' framing — as if the tea run is a miraculous coincidence rather than something she just requested — is peak Moira. This lands especially well because the name is wrong.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. Are you saying that I could be implicated in this?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, darling, the courts will decide.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, no, no. That's not comforting!

jokes.text: Moira: 'I'm sorry, David, I had nowhere else to turn. It's probably nothing, but I think I've killed a man.'
jokes.setup: Moira has come to David's store in distress, claiming she had 'nowhere else to turn'
jokes.punchline: 'It's probably nothing, but I think I've killed a man.' — the casual minimizing ('probably nothing') attached to MURDER makes it hilarious
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'It's probably nothing, but I think I've killed a man.' The structure is perfect — the minimizing preface ('probably nothing') completely undercuts the gravity of what follows. Only Moira would frame possible manslaughter as 'probably nothing.' The character integration is exceptional — this is exactly how Moira would deliver news of this magnitude. Earns its 8+ score.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, you're making me feel worse about this. I feel like you've just pushed me off a ledge.

jokes.text: Moira: 'The last time I felt this emotionally encumbered, I was playing Lady Macbeth on a Crystal Skies cruise ship during Shakespeare at Sea Week.'
jokes.setup: Moira explains her current level of emotional distress
jokes.punchline: Her emotional reference point is a theatrical performance on a cruise ship — the specific detail of 'Crystal Skies' cruise line and 'Shakespeare at Sea Week' elevates the absurdity
jokes.explanation: The specificity does the work here — not just 'a cruise ship' but 'Crystal Skies cruise ship during Shakespeare at Sea Week.' This level of specific absurdism is Schitt's Creek at its best. Also: comparing current stress to playing LADY MACBETH is both self-aggrandizing and, given the situation, oddly appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh my God. I'm going to prison. I'm going to prison and I'm going to have to share a cell with someone named Brenda who has a teardrop tattoo and a gambling problem.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And she's going to ask me to do her commissary, and I won't know what that means, and she's going to think I'm being difficult.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And then one day she's going to ask me to make a shank out of a toothbrush, and I'm going to accidentally make a very chic hair accessory instead, and she's going to stab me with it.

jokes.text: Patrick returns with perfectly customized drinks including 'one caramel macchiato skim, 2 sweeteners, and a sprinkle of cocoa powder' for David
jokes.setup: Patrick went to get Moira a tea
jokes.punchline: Patrick has memorized David's exact complex coffee order and got it without being asked — showing both his attentiveness and the absurd specificity of David's preferences
jokes.explanation: This is a romantic comedy beat executed through comedy — Patrick's attentiveness is sweet AND funny. The extremely specific order ('2 sweeteners, sprinkle of cocoa powder') characterizes David perfectly. The rewatch bonus is because knowing the David/Patrick arc makes this more touching on rewatch.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, he's like a golden retriever in a cashmere sweater.

jokes.text: Moira to Patrick: 'You're very speedy.' Patrick: 'Thanks. I wasn't sure about the scone, so I got one just to be safe.' Moira: 'There's nothing wrong with treating yourself, dear.'
jokes.setup: Patrick returns quickly with everything; Moira compliments his speed
jokes.punchline: Patrick got a scone 'just to be safe' (a practical hedge for Moira's contradictory ordering); Moira accepts this as wisdom about self-indulgence rather than acknowledging her own confusion
jokes.explanation: Patrick's pragmatic 'I wasn't sure about the scone, so I got one just to be safe' is a gentle callback to Moira's ordering confusion. Moira reinterpreting this as a treatise on self-care is a nice character beat. Solid but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We'd love for you and your child to join us for happy hour.

dialogue.speaker: Guest
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, what?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's just drinks. Well, for us. You can get your kid a Shirley Temple or something.

dialogue.speaker: Guest
dialogue.line: You want me to bring my 8-year-old to a bar?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Is that not... appropriate?

jokes.text: David fast-forwards Moira's backstory: 'Um, I woke up yesterday morning with a spring in my step.' / 'Start later, please.' / '...I could never know where the day was...' / 'Fast forward it.'
jokes.setup: Moira is finally asked to explain how she 'killed a man' and starts with 'how to get started'
jokes.punchline: Moira begins with 'I woke up yesterday morning with a spring in my step' — David's increasingly terse interruptions ('Start later, please' / 'Fast forward it') as Moira keeps beginning from irrelevant points
jokes.explanation: The escalating directorial interruptions ('Start later, please' → 'Fast forward it') while Moira keeps picking irrelevant starting points is excellent character comedy. The rhythm builds nicely and the contrast between David's urgency and Moira's meandering is very well-constructed.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, children love rocks. They're very popular with children. A rock is like... it's like a toy that costs nothing and requires no batteries.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You can throw it, you can skip it, you can build things with it. It's basically the original iPhone.

jokes.text: Moira refused to give a dying man a painkiller because she was embarrassed to admit she was 'hoarding... sample packets of a basic headache medication'
jokes.setup: Moira gave an old man a headache pill that she denied having, and now he's dead
jokes.punchline: The reason she withheld it: shame over hoarding pharmacy sample packets — a petty, absurd reason that may have contributed to a man's death
jokes.explanation: The revelation of Moira's true motivation is perfectly character-specific — her pride and vanity overriding basic human compassion over something as mundane as free sample packets. That she calls them 'complimentary bolus' (using a medical term) in the next beat makes it even richer.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm going to give rocks to children.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh my God, that's the best idea I've ever heard!

jokes.text: Moira: 'This is what my life has come to, David, killing a man over a complimentary bolus.'
jokes.setup: Moira has explained her reason for withholding the painkiller
jokes.punchline: 'Complimentary bolus' — using medical terminology ('bolus') for free pharmacy samples, framing her downfall in the most dramatic possible terms
jokes.explanation: 'Complimentary bolus' is wonderfully specific — 'bolus' is a real medical term meaning a dose of medication, which makes Moira's use of it both pretentious and weirdly accurate. The combination of 'complimentary' (implying something free/trivial) with 'bolus' (clinical) is perfect Moira vocabulary. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: No, you don't have to think about it. It's mandatory.

jokes.text: David: 'Well, know that you've unloaded this on me, what am I, an accomplice?' Moira: 'David, I came here to be talked off a ledge, not pushed!'
jokes.setup: David panics at the idea of being implicated in Moira's potential manslaughter
jokes.punchline: Moira's retort — 'I came here to be talked off a ledge, not pushed!' — is a perfect metaphor delivered with theatrical indignation
jokes.explanation: 'I came here to be talked off a ledge, not pushed!' is sharp and quotable — a perfect expression of Moira's sense of betrayal when David doesn't immediately provide comfort. The metaphor is vivid and works both literally and figuratively. The rhythm is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, it's not mandatory. I mean, it's strongly suggested.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Mandatory.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: There's actually a fee if you don't attend.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Can you imagine this in prison?' — worried about being incarcerated for not sharing a Tylenol.
jokes.setup: Moira is spiraling about potential legal consequences for not giving the dead man a headache tablet.
jokes.punchline: The leap from 'didn't share a free pharmacy sample' to 'prison' is enormous and Moira makes it with complete sincerity — her prison concern is presumably about her personal comfort, not the justice of it.
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Moira's spiral. 'Can you imagine this in prison?' is a line that invites the audience to imagine Moira in prison — which is funny — and implies she's thought about it enough to be genuinely concerned.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Making it economically mandatory instead

jokes.text: Patrick interjects having 'accidentally' overheard: 'I know I wasn't supposed to be listening to this conversation, but it's a small space, and your voice carries so beautifully.'
jokes.setup: Moira has been loudly confessing to possible manslaughter in a small shop
jokes.punchline: Patrick's polite excuse for having listened ('your voice carries so beautifully') is both a genuine compliment and a hilariously diplomatic way of saying she was impossible to ignore
jokes.explanation: Patrick's delivery here is delicious — the compliment ('so beautifully') neutralizes what could be an accusation of eavesdropping while also being objectively true. It's a perfect Patrick line: polite, slightly amused, deftly diplomatic. The character integration is strong.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I've noticed that nobody in this town locks their doors. Like, at all. I went to three job interviews today, and I just walked into all of them without knocking. One woman didn't even look up from her desk.

jokes.text: Patrick explains Moira is legally protected by not giving the man medication, resolving her crisis. Moira: 'Well there we have it then.' David: 'Thank God you're here Pa—' Moira simultaneously: '—trick.'
jokes.setup: David is about to thank Patrick by name for saving the day
jokes.punchline: David and Moira both complete 'trick' simultaneously — David finishing 'Patrick,' Moira presumably having been about to say something else, with both landing on the same syllable unexpectedly
jokes.explanation: The staging of both David and Moira landing on '—trick' simultaneously is a nice visual/verbal gag, with the implication that Moira has now been corrected on Patrick's name. Works as a callback to the Peter/Patrick confusion.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Can I stay at your place for a few days?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Oh, um, actually that's not going to work. I'm seeing someone.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What? Since when?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Since last month. Her name is Heather.

jokes.text: Moira: 'I suppose I'll head back to the scene of the crime, with which I had nothing to do.'
jokes.setup: Patrick has just legally exonerated Moira
jokes.punchline: Moira's conspicuously over-clarified 'with which I had nothing to do' — the innocent person's classic self-incriminating over-explanation
jokes.explanation: The compulsive over-clarification ('with which I had nothing to do') is exactly what a guilty-feeling innocent person does. It's the comedic equivalent of 'I'm not nervous, why would I be nervous?' Solid character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: It's just that my roommate has a very strict no overnight guests policy, and I don't think he would be comfortable with—

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You don't want me to stay over.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, no, I mean, yes, but it's not about you, it's just—

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You literally just said your roommate won't allow it.

jokes.text: Patrick tries to charge Moira for the tea; Moira: 'I paid way too much for a wedge of brie last week, so let's call it even.' Patrick: 'I don't think it works like that.'
jokes.setup: Patrick rings up Moira's tea
jokes.punchline: Moira invents a barter economy based on past overpriced brie — attempting to cash in a prior grievance as currency for a current purchase
jokes.explanation: This is a brilliantly absurd economic logic — Moira has invented a personal grievance offset system. The specificity of 'a wedge of brie' makes it funnier. Patrick's patient 'I don't think it works like that' is the perfect straight-man response. Very quotable and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, so Ted's dating Heather? That girl goes through men like I go through... well, like I go through everything. Quickly and without thinking about the consequences.

jokes.text: Moira exits, passing comment: 'Alexis was right, he's a button.' (about Patrick)
jokes.setup: Alexis had earlier called Patrick a 'sweet little button face'
jokes.punchline: Moira independently arrives at the same assessment — 'he's a button' — condensing Alexis's phrase, suggesting the descriptor has made its way through the Rose family
jokes.explanation: A satisfying callback that's also efficient — 'he's a button' distills Alexis's longer phrase into Moira's more economical delivery. The callback works because it confirms both the family's communication (Moira knew Alexis said it) and their united assessment of Patrick. Good warmth here.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: So who would you hire?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, they both have their strengths. One person has a Harvard MBA and 20 years of experience in finance, and the other person is really good at Instagram.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Those are not equivalent qualifications.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know, but like, the Instagram person is really, really good at it.

jokes.text: John and Roland knock on guest rooms to invite them to 'happy hour' — actually to keep them distracted from the coroner arriving
jokes.setup: John needs to keep guests occupied between 4-5pm when the coroner will remove the body
jokes.punchline: The 'happy hour' is a cover operation, and John and Roland attempting to be cheerful motel hosts while concealing a dead body in one of the rooms is inherently farcical
jokes.explanation: The situational irony of the cover operation is the engine of this scene rather than any individual line. Functional setup for the jokes to follow.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: When I kissed you, it felt like my first kiss. Like, actually my first kiss. Everything I did before that... I don't know what that was.

jokes.text: Guest: 'You're inviting my son to happy hour?' John: 'Well, wrong choice of words.'
jokes.setup: John has invited a mother and her child to 'happy hour'
jokes.punchline: The obvious problem — 'happy hour' implies alcohol — pointed out by the mother; John's immediate concession 'wrong choice of words' is admirably honest
jokes.explanation: A reliable setup/punchline on the obvious problem with inviting children to 'happy hour.' John's quick 'wrong choice of words' is self-aware but not particularly inventive. Functional.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I have made some poor romantic choices in my past.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: By poor, I mean catastrophically bad.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I once dated someone who collected porcelain dolls.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Not as a hobby. As a lifestyle.

jokes.text: John tries to sell the child on 'happy hour' by noting a quarry worker is attending: 'I bet he has a lot of stories... and what kind of kid doesn't like rocks? Maybe he'll even get to take one home.'
jokes.setup: The mother is skeptical about bringing her young child to 'happy hour'
jokes.punchline: John's increasingly desperate pitch — a quarry worker, his 'stories,' rocks as entertainment — represents an adorably hopeless attempt to make motel imprisonment sound fun for a child
jokes.explanation: 'What kind of kid doesn't like rocks?' is an absurdly sincere non-sequitur. The escalating desperation — 'stories'... 'rocks'... 'maybe he'll even get to take one home' — builds nicely. The rock promise as a children's event perk is the kind of small-town mundane detail Schitt's Creek excels at.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Can you just call me nice?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: Mother agrees to 'think about it.' John: 'Yeah, it's mandatory.'
jokes.setup: The mother says she'll 'think about it' regarding the happy hour
jokes.punchline: John instinctively blurts 'Yeah, it's mandatory' — immediately revealing the coercive nature of the 'invitation'
jokes.explanation: The reveal of 'mandatory' after a long hospitable pitch is a great comedic reversal. John's anxiety about the situation slipping out in two words is perfectly timed. The brevity is ideal — any more words would ruin it. This earns a solid score for structure and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, the cinnamon buns are sweet, and the vodka is... vodka. Together they create a kind of balance. It's actually quite sophisticated when you think about it.

jokes.text: John's recovery: 'Well again, wrong choice of words, it's not mandatory, but there is a fee for not attending, so you'll be paying for it either way.'
jokes.setup: John just said happy hour is 'mandatory' and is trying to walk it back
jokes.punchline: His correction is somehow worse — 'not mandatory, but there is a fee for not attending' — creating a de facto mandatory event with financial coercion
jokes.explanation: The correction that's actually worse than the original is a classic comedy escalation. 'There is a fee for not attending' is legally absurd and logically equivalent to mandatory. The guest's 'What?' reaction is merited. Well-constructed bit that builds on the previous beat.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Is anyone here from out of town?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh. Right. Of course you are. We all are.

jokes.text: John and Roland tell the guest to make sure her son is 'up and ready to go at 3:55' — a suspiciously precise time, clearly chosen to preempt the 4pm body removal.
jokes.setup: They're wrapping up the coercive 'family hour' invite.
jokes.punchline: The hyper-specific '3:55' time reveals the operational desperation behind the social performance — they need the guests corralled before 4pm sharp.
jokes.explanation: The 3:55 specificity is a nice detail that reveals the planning without explaining it. Functional situational joke.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Did you bring rocks for the kids?

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: What? No. Why would I bring rocks for the kids?

jokes.text: Ted: 'I have to admit, Alexis, some of your questions were shockingly effective. I found it very telling how many people would just accept a cocktail from a total stranger.'
jokes.setup: The job interviews are over; Ted is evaluating the session
jokes.punchline: Ted's backhanded compliment implies Alexis's 'effective' interview questions were unconventional (offering drinks to see who accepts) and reveals something unflattering about the applicants AND about Alexis's interviewing style
jokes.explanation: The idea that Alexis was handing out cocktails during job interviews and Ted found this 'telling' is an offscreen joke that works well. Ted's genuine appreciation mixed with obvious bewilderment is good character texture.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I mean, we should all be cherishing every moment we're alive, because you never know when you're going to end up like Gerald, face down in a bowl of French onion soup.

jokes.text: Ted: 'Who would've known that Paul was allergic to cats?' Alexis: 'What was he doing here?'
jokes.setup: Ted and Alexis are reviewing the interview results
jokes.punchline: Alexis's immediate response to learning Paul is allergic to cats — the central qualification for a vet clinic job — is not surprise but confusion about why he was there at all
jokes.explanation: 'What was he doing here?' is Alexis at her best — her confusion is completely logical (why would a cat-allergic person apply at a vet?) but delivered as if it's the most obvious question in the world. The question she asks is reasonable, which makes the comedic beat subtler and funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We don't know when we're going to die. We could go at any moment. I could be implicated in a death, and then cleared of that death.

jokes.text: Ted: 'You definitely had a certain something, and I can't quite put my finger on [it].'
jokes.setup: Ted is telling Alexis it'll be different without her
jokes.punchline: Ted's inability to articulate what Alexis's 'certain something' was — the implication being it may not have been entirely professional
jokes.explanation: A gentle ironic beat. Ted's diplomatic vagueness ('certain something... can't quite put my finger on') is both honest and evasive. It works but doesn't land as a strong laugh — more of a warm character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What happens in Vegas stays in Vegas. What happens in our house also stays in our house because we can't afford to replace anything.

jokes.text: Ted reveals he has 'a date tonight' and 'may need the apartment at some point' — Alexis's dawning realization: 'My God, like for sex.'
jokes.setup: Alexis asks to crash at Ted's place; Ted awkwardly explains he can't, hedging about 'needing the apartment'
jokes.punchline: Alexis's blunt completion of Ted's implication: 'My God, like for sex.' — delivered with the stunned clarity of someone who just figured out a complex puzzle
jokes.explanation: 'My God, like for sex.' is a great line — the 'My God' implies genuine shock, as if sex was an outlandish possibility, and the 'like for sex' is refreshingly direct. Alexis's stunned realization played as a dramatic breakthrough moment about something obvious is peak character comedy.

jokes.text: Ted's date is revealed to be 'Heather, the woman with the cat' — Alexis: 'Yes, the woman who wanted you to wash her cat all the time.'
jokes.setup: Ted explains he's dating Heather
jokes.punchline: Alexis identifies Heather entirely by the detail of wanting her cat washed — implying this was a defining characteristic she found strange or excessive
jokes.explanation: 'The woman who wanted you to wash her cat all the time' is a specific, funny detail. Alexis reducing Heather to this single strange habit is good character comedy and implies Alexis had opinions about this even during their working relationship.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'I don't know why I thought you were single.'
jokes.setup: Ted has revealed he's been seeing someone — 'Heather, the woman with the cat' — who is now a more serious girlfriend.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's bafflement at her own assumption — she apparently had no idea Ted was in a relationship despite having worked with him — reveals her complete absorption in herself.
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in Alexis's obliviousness — she genuinely didn't register that Ted was dating someone. 'I don't know why I thought you were single' is self-aware enough to be charming rather than purely narcissistic.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Hmm. Did. Well, good for Heather.' Then, after a pause: 'Thirsty little thing, isn't she.'
jokes.setup: Ted explains his casual thing with Heather 'turned into more of a thing'
jokes.punchline: Alexis's layered response — the clipped 'Hmm. Did. Well, good for Heather.' followed by the devastating afterthought 'Thirsty little thing, isn't she.' — reveals jealousy masked by breezy unconcern
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional character comedy. The structure is perfect: the first beat ('Well, good for Heather') is pointed but controlled. Then — after a pause — 'Thirsty little thing, isn't she.' The afterthought delivery is the key; Alexis can't quite suppress her true feelings. 'Thirsty' is both casual and savage. The economy is excellent. This earns the 8+ score.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Thirsty little thing, isn't she.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is processing that Ted's casual relationship with Heather escalated into something serious.
jokes.punchline: 'Thirsty little thing' applied to Heather — for presumably pursuing a serious relationship with Ted — is Alexis's competitive instinct expressing itself through a backhanded observation.
jokes.explanation: Alexis jealousy expressed as mild shade. 'Thirsty little thing' is a memorably specific Alexis-ism — critical enough to reveal her feelings, phrased charmingly enough to maintain plausible deniability.

jokes.text: When asked who to hire, Alexis recommends: 'Um, I liked the guy with the glasses.' Then: 'Um, or the guy with cystic acne. Either or.' Then: 'Or the girl with the oily braid. Also very good.'
jokes.setup: Ted asks Alexis who she'd hire from the interviews
jokes.punchline: Alexis's three candidates are identified entirely by unfortunate physical characteristics — glasses, cystic acne, oily braid — revealing that she retained nothing professional about any of them
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'glasses' (relatively neutral) to 'cystic acne' to 'oily braid' is perfectly calibrated. Alexis clearly has no retention of anything professional and categorizes humans entirely by physical quirks. 'Either or' and 'Also very good' as closing endorsements are delightfully breezy. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: David's panicked opener: 'So you told your mom about us, huh.' Patrick, correcting: 'No, I told Alexis about us, and she likely told my mom.'
jokes.setup: Patrick seems to think David disclosed their relationship to Moira
jokes.punchline: David's correction — Alexis is the source, and Moira is downstream — establishing the exact information flow within the Rose family gossip network
jokes.explanation: A light character beat. The implication that David automatically told Alexis who automatically told Moira is a nice bit of family dynamics texture. Not a big laugh but it's efficient character work.

jokes.text: David explains his sleepover request: 'it was purely circumstantial, you know, because of the dead body at the—' Patrick: 'Right.'
jokes.setup: David is explaining his innocent motive for wanting to stay at Patrick's place
jokes.punchline: The mid-sentence appearance of 'dead body' as a casual explanatory clause — 'purely circumstantial, you know, because of the dead body at the—' — is a reminder that there is genuinely a corpse at the motel
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the casual insertion of 'dead body' into a sincere romantic conversation. The trailing 'at the—' as David stops himself (presumably Patrick cuts him off or it's obvious) is nicely economical. Patrick's 'Right.' perfectly mirrors the earlier structural beat of John's 'Hmm.'

jokes.text: David: 'Well, if we're being honest with each other, this is sort of like my first time, too. I mean it's not, I've kissed like a thousand people but... nobody that I... cared about or... respected... or thought was nice.'
jokes.setup: Patrick has shared a vulnerable moment about the kiss feeling like a first
jokes.punchline: David's reciprocal vulnerability is immediately undermined by the parenthetical 'I've kissed like a thousand people' — and then deepened by 'nobody that I cared about or respected or thought was nice'
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best moments because it's both genuinely touching AND hilarious simultaneously. 'I've kissed like a thousand people but... nobody that I cared about or respected or thought was nice' — the escalating self-condemnation (cared about → respected → THOUGHT WAS NICE) is perfectly structured. 'Thought was nice' as the baseline floor of his past relationships is devastating and funny. The character integration is near-perfect — this is exactly David's combination of bravado and vulnerability. Earns 8+.

jokes.text: David and Patrick argue over 'nice' vs 'good': 'I just need you to say nice person.' 'You're a good person.' 'That's not nice.'
jokes.setup: Patrick has said he thinks David is a 'good person'; David corrects him — he said 'nice person'
jokes.punchline: Patrick keeps saying 'good' and David keeps correcting — a semantic standoff that's both funny and emotionally revealing (David needs 'nice' specifically)
jokes.explanation: The semantic disagreement over 'nice' vs 'good' is a callback to David's earlier confession ('nobody that I... thought was nice'). Patrick saying 'good' instead and David's insistence on 'nice' is both funny and emotionally loaded. The repetition works perfectly. 'That's not nice' as a punchline is quietly great.

jokes.text: John opens the motel 'happy hour' by welcoming guests to 'the cinnamon buns and vodka' spread, describing it as 'festive in a... Scandinavian sort of way'
jokes.setup: John is hosting an emergency happy hour to keep guests distracted from the coroner
jokes.punchline: The combination of cinnamon buns and vodka is inherently strange, and John's cultural justification — 'Scandinavian' — is a desperately improvised explanation that makes it funnier
jokes.explanation: Cinnamon buns and vodka is a genuinely funny combination and the 'Scandinavian' justification is a perfect example of John improvising confidence he doesn't have. The ellipsis before 'Scandinavian' implies he's making it up in real time. Solid absurdist humor grounded in character.

jokes.text: John: 'We thought it was festive in a... Scandinavian sort of way.'
jokes.setup: The cinnamon buns and vodka combination is unusual refreshment for a motel 'family hour.' John senses this needs explanation.
jokes.punchline: John's reach for 'Scandinavian' as the cultural cover for vodka-and-pastry at a mandatory motel gathering is inspired improvisation. The pause before 'Scandinavian' reveals him arriving at the excuse in real time.
jokes.explanation: A good ad-lib energy to the line. John is clearly making this up and the ellipsis before 'Scandinavian' implies real-time invention. Pairs well with the previous joke.

jokes.text: John: 'Anyone from out of town?' [pause] 'Well, that makes sense. You're all staying at the motel.'
jokes.setup: John is trying to facilitate conversation among the captive audience of motel guests
jokes.punchline: His ice-breaker question — 'anyone from out of town?' — is answered by silence (obviously yes, they're all at a motel) and John's recognition of his own question's absurdity
jokes.explanation: The self-undermining ice-breaker is a classic comedy beat executed well here. The silence following 'anyone from out of town?' at a motel is the punchline, and John's gentle self-correction ('well, that makes sense') shows awareness without embarrassment. Good deadpan.

jokes.text: John: 'Frank, Frank... Frank is here. Frank who works at a quarry. Did you bring some rocks in for the kids, Frank?' Frank: 'No, I didn't bring any rocks.' John: 'No rocks? Well.'
jokes.setup: John had promised a guest's child that Frank the quarry worker might bring rocks
jokes.punchline: Frank has not brought rocks — a plan that was always absurd — and John's deflated 'No rocks? Well.' is a perfect expression of a man whose improvised plan has collapsed
jokes.explanation: The callback to the 'rocks' promise pays off perfectly. 'No rocks? Well.' is an ideal three-word defeat. John's earlier confidence in Frank's rock-bringing capacity was always going to end here. The cringe discomfort of John's collapsing cover story is satisfying.

jokes.text: Moira arrives at the happy hour: 'Isn't this a glittering sea of hopeful faces.' — addressing the trapped, confused guests
jokes.setup: Moira arrives at John's emergency damage-control party
jokes.punchline: 'Glittering sea of hopeful faces' applied to people who are clearly confused, stuck at a motel's impromptu happy hour, being corralled away from a dead body
jokes.explanation: Moira's theatrical address to what are clearly bewildered, trapped strangers is great character comedy. 'Glittering sea of hopeful faces' is deliciously overwrought vocabulary for motel guests at a mandatory happy hour.

jokes.text: Guest: 'We wanna go outside.' Moira: 'Well of course you do, who doesn't want to go outside? But it's also important to cherish what's going on inside.'
jokes.setup: Guests want to leave the lobby; Moira must keep them inside (because the coroner is outside)
jokes.punchline: Moira's profound-sounding deflection — 'it's also important to cherish what's going on inside' — is simultaneously pseudo-philosophical nonsense and a desperate lie
jokes.explanation: Moira's quasi-inspirational hostage-keeping is peak situational irony. The words 'what's going on inside' are technically about their inner emotional lives but are actually about keeping them away from the coroner. The double meaning is well-executed.

jokes.text: Moira: 'It's times like these that reminds one of the fact that we're all still alive. Don't you dare take that for granted. One day you're asking for an anti-inflammatory, the next day you've passed away in your sleep!'
jokes.setup: Moira is supposed to be keeping guests entertained and unaware of the death
jokes.punchline: Moira delivers a semi-unhinged speech about death and mortality — directly referencing the exact scenario of the dead guest — while ostensibly running a cheerful hospitality event
jokes.explanation: Moira's inability to suppress the actual event she's suppressing is great comedy. She works through her own guilt about the painkiller incident by essentially describing it to the people she's supposed to be distracting. 'One day you're asking for an anti-inflammatory' is a specific callback to her own situation. The obliviousness to how inappropriate this is for the context is perfect character work.

jokes.text: John interrupting Moira's death speech: 'I think we're getting a little off-topic, sweetheart.'
jokes.setup: Moira has delivered an unsolicited speech about mortality and death to the trapped guests
jokes.punchline: John's gentle, masterful understatement — 'a little off-topic' — for what is actually a crisis of Moira nearly confessing everything
jokes.explanation: John's 'a little off-topic, sweetheart' is the perfect spousal deflection. The economy is great — 'a little' does enormous understatement work. John's long-practiced ability to gently redirect Moira from public disasters is a running character dynamic.

jokes.text: Moira: 'The point being, no one knows when we're going to die! Or be implicated in, and then suddenly cleared of, someone else's death.'
jokes.setup: John has tried to get Moira back on topic; Moira pushes through to her point
jokes.punchline: 'No one knows when we're going to die! Or be implicated in, and then suddenly cleared of, someone else's death.' — Moira has essentially confessed her entire arc of the episode in a toast
jokes.explanation: This is a near-perfect episode button. Moira's philosophical address to the guests manages to summarize her entire secret storyline — the guilt, the investigation, the exoneration — while remaining just oblique enough that the guests probably don't follow. 'Or be implicated in, and then suddenly cleared of, someone else's death' is a masterful extension of a universal life-truth that is specific only to Moira. The structure is excellent — the universal premise ('no one knows when we die') followed by the extremely specific personal corollary. This earns 8+.

jokes.text: Moira wraps up: 'So, so, drink up! Go outside! And live your lives! And thank you all for spending this hour with us.'
jokes.setup: Moira has delivered a bizarre death-and-exoneration toast
jokes.punchline: Her transition from existential crisis speech to cheerful 'drink up! Go outside!' is a whiplash tonal shift that only Moira could execute with sincerity
jokes.explanation: The tonal whiplash from mortality meditation to 'drink up!' is characteristically Moira. The 'thank you all for spending this hour with us' treats a forced detainment as a voluntary social engagement. Good character-consistent comedy.

jokes.text: John's final instruction to departing guests: 'People, let's leave the cups inside. We're not in Vegas, here.'
jokes.setup: Guests are finally being released from the mandatory happy hour
jokes.punchline: John's very specific anti-Vegas etiquette reminder — implying people were actually about to take their cups outside, and distinguishing Schitt's Creek from Las Vegas
jokes.explanation: A strong final beat. 'We're not in Vegas, here.' is a great tag line for the episode — the contrast between the Roses' aspirations and their actual circumstances crystallized in one Vegas comparison. Also implicitly funny that John has thought enough about this to have a cups policy.

tmdb_overview: While Moira fears she is responsible for the death of a motel guest, David and Patrick decide to make their budding romance official.
insight: Season 4 opens strong: 61 jokes in 23 minutes, deadpan escalation carries the premiere.
